My name is Simon Palmer

In 2006, our son was born with a rare chromosome condition,
meaning he is fully dependent for every aspect of his life.
I struggle to understand the fairness of the Triple PR Act
that discriminates by imposing an annual audit cost.
Financially disadvantaging only those individuals
whom have a disability.
I'm sure you understand that this cohort are unable
to manage their own affairs and make their own decisions.
They are the most vulnerable individuals in our community.
They need the greatest level of care, support
and protection, most of whom earn the least.
Property managers currently don't have the ability to engage
with an independent auditor
and as a result have no control over the
associated auditing costs.
There are no upper limits to what can be charged.
I therefore don't believe the act is fully
protecting the subject person.
This is potentially exposing these individuals
to exploitation through the charge app model
that is currently being used.
The corporate charge out model that public Trust uses
is well recognized across the corporate
and business sector within New Zealand.
Time and attendance is charged at a rate of $247 per hour
for any portion of a six minute unit.
Detailed on a job sheet I requested from Public Trust
for my son's first audit were six separate six minute units.
One of those units was a phone call I made to public trust
that lasted no more than two minutes
and this calculates out at an actual charge out rate
of $740 per hour.
Uh, likewise there was a six minute allocation for the
generated invoice charge.
It's highly unlikely that this would've taken the
full six minutes.
This model always results in time spent, rounded up
to the nearest six minute unit,
resulting in an actual charge out rate significantly higher
than the $247 per hour rate.
This model does not reflect service cost
or cost, uh, recovery methodology as required to be used
by public organizations
and is generating profit for the public trust
and in turn a dividend to the government.
Any government seeking
to profit from this cohort demonstrates a lack
of understanding and care as a corporate model.
Public trust also deals with overdue payments
noted on an overdue account I received was, please be aware
if this invoice is not paid with them 14 days,
we may have no option
but to take further steps to recover the outstanding debt.
This action may incur collection costs
and could negatively affect your credit rating.
That's my son's credit rating.
Public trust also offer payment plans for those
that can't afford to pay the full amount.
A payment plan for an imposed cost
that the protected person never asked for
and obviously can't afford.
Public trust have advised
that unpaid property manager examination fees have increased
significantly since the subsidy was removed in October, 2024
and sits as of the 31st of January 26 at
This corporate model is well understood
and accepted by the corporate business sector
of New Zealand, however, is not appropriate for this cohort.
This corporate model is exploiting the most
vulnerable individuals.
The model is imposed on low income individuals
that would not otherwise engage with corporate services.
As these services for this income group
are simply unaffordable as the $352,000
of unpaid fees indicate.

of training and support resulting in a cost
to the protected person.
Property managers are moms

or business backgrounds

for auditing both challenging and stressful.
Limited guidance is available
however, no training is provided.

and fear of the protected person being charged.
As I've already mentioned,
the auditing fee discriminates against
those with disabilities.
No individual should be subjected to an imposed cost
because of how they were born.
The Triple PR act not only covers orders
for property managers part four,
but enduring power of attorney part nine.
Under part four, the property manager must file a set
of annual accounts as I do,
and the protected person is charged the associated fee
under part nine, an enduring power of attorney.
There is no obligation
for the person managing the donor's property
to file financial accounts
and subsequently no fees, no audit fees.
This inconsistency is at odds with the intent of the act
to provide protection to the most vulnerable individuals.
It discriminates against a cohort
by once again financially disadvantaging them
through the auditing process and the associated fees
and another co cohort by not providing any protection.
To conclude, there appears to be a true lack
of understanding and care for this cohort
and this supporting communities driven by budget constraints
and during a cost of living crisis.
In October, 2024, the current government
with limited understanding through insufficient consultation
with the disability sector removed the subsidy
that was available to this cohort.
They increased both the asset threshold to 25,000
and the charge out rate to $247 per hour.
Those who now sit below the new $25,000 asset limit
and income thresholds no longer have protection.
The undetected misappropriation of funds
for these individuals would have a significant impact in
long-term ramifications.
Professional advice I received was to spend my son's money

This is advice I would not give to a non-disabled person,

With unpaid fees increasing 250%

these latest settings are clearly not working
and are simply unaffordable.

It's a thankless job.
Day in, day out 24 7, my wife

compared to their benefit income.

as I understand it under the Public Trust annual reports,

of Justice is providing in this space

and I quote reasons of affordability do not prevent

other people have talked about trying to work it so they get

Um, and that's really concerning.
Um, some of these, some
of the subject people receiving these invoices, um,
actually now qualify as a result of the lower, as a result
of the lifting of those thresholds for,
um, property management orders.
Um, they would now actually, um, qualify
for property administration, which means they wouldn't have
to have their, um, accounts examined
and they wouldn't have to pay public
trust examination requirements.
And, um, at this stage though,
because they are still under property management orders, um,
they have to pay these invoices.
Um, but they weren't given an opportunity to transition

people into these other kinds of orders

will probably speak to that.

where a subject person now has assets over the new

I speak in our submission about, um,
one community law center that's aware of a case
where the subject person's Kiwi gave a balance,
sits over the new actual threshold asset threshold of 25,000
because their family was putting money aside so
that they could be cared for in their later years.
And now that person is getting, has to pay out
of their welfare benefit
because they're over this threshold.

that the subject person is gonna have
to carry potentially for decades.
They don't get access to KiwiSaver
for at least another 20 years or so.
Um, so there's, there's all kinds
of different complex ways in which this in fact situation in
which this issue arises.
So our recommendation is that there should be a subsidy
or some sort of funding for these examination fees
to cover all people, and particularly people, um,
under property management orders who are on benefits.
Um, you know, they might have, uh, a house sitting there
as an older person or they might have, uh,
they like the person I talked about who's younger
and they are on a, on a social welfare, um, benefit
for their day-to-day living and 500 to a thousand dollars.
We've seen a thousands, we've seen a, a fee of a thousand.
You know, this is not small money.
So our recommendation is that if there is, if there has
to be some, if, if the subsidy's not gonna be reintroduced
or there's not gonna be any funding, then the people

that threshold should at least have theirs waived

through the Ministry of Justice and the public trust.
There must be a way of transitioning these people if they
want to be to a property property administration order so

Um, I really, um, am pleased that the Ministry

on adult decision making.
Uh, it highlights that most, if not all people
that are subject to these orders are just, oh,
they say most if not all people are sub,
that are subject under these orders are disabled.
And that charging fees for recovering the cost

And I've seen, um, others refer to this as a disability tax.
I really encourage, um, the, um, petitions committee
and CLCA, uh, recommends

of requiring people to pay.
So, um, I'm gonna hand over to Maryanne now.
Thanks to Maryanne for joining us.
She's just gonna talk through, um, a couple
of scenarios that she's seen.
Thanks Karen. Well welcome Maryanne.
Thanks for the invitation to speak.
It's, um, great to be able
to talk on a topic that is very passionate.
To me it's the majority of my work is working with people
or organizations trying to navigate the legal systems, uh,
when someone's lost capacity or,
or already has lost capacity.

of attorney area as well.
So I know both sides, uh, when it comes to capacity issues.
Um, so yeah, daily I see people
who can't see a private lawyer, they don't have the ability

Um, they're often highly stressed when they come to see me

Um, so, but what's happened now is on top of navigating

Uh, and I said, well, funnily enough,
I'm having a chat next week, so, um,
I'll raise your issue as well.
Um, so she's saying that basically they can get nobody

which is really concerning, um,
because you've got already got vulnerable people there.
Uh, and now, you know,
in some cases they're left unprotected,
they obviously don't have powers of attorney.
Uh, and now they can't get court orders

Um, so they then, then, you know,
potentially they're unmonitored as well.
You end up potentially

Admittedly, some family members have been reluctant anyway

of reluctance to apply.
Um, so yeah, for cases for me on the ground,
I've seen couples in, in the family home

still the rates insurance all on their sole pension.
Um, or in one case I've got one woman
that she's working 60 hours a week, so she's paying

that's almost $20 a week effectively from his $56
is going to cover off an audit fee.
So almost a third of his income.
Um, and,
and one other case, I've got a client owns the home
with the spouse that lives here

they don't really have the ability to pay pay these funds.

that Mr. Palmer has made.

We've got time for a couple quick questions.
Uh, yeah, thank you.

and the, the, the essential issue if I,
if I've heard you guys
and anyone else, right, is that the, uh, the request is
that people not be charged personally
for the audit cost, correct?
Correct. Okay.
And, and, and if it has to be there, there needs to be some,
some way of stepping, like
not have it either everybody doesn't have to pay for these,
um, or there's some way of excluding certain people.
Gotcha. Okay. Is, is there, um, is there any, um,

um, of, of abuse and exploitation?

The audit is statutorily required,
the public trust under the Triple

I'm just meaning we're, we're, if we're asking for a review

I I would say a hundred percent it is required.
I have organizations again with elder abuse contracts

Um, most people are, are very well-meaning

but there are definitely problems out there, um, with, um,

Um, but that's not just across, um,

there's a lot of that going unchecked.
Um, and a really,
But sorry, but, but,
but Enduring Power's attorney are

Oh, that's separate.
That's correct. But, But you would like to see auditing

source_metadata.description:
Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988

Simon Palmer

Ministry of Justice - 

Caroline Greaney, Deputy Secretary Policy
